Short answer  
• Perception of the world is indeed a story your brain tells, but that does not mean the world (or mathematics) is arbitrary fiction.  
• What the “editor” freely rewrites are mainly episodic details and feelings.  Semantic knowledge—facts, vocabulary, multiplication tables—gets stored and maintained in a more rigid, redundancy-heavy way.  
• Because humans know personal memory is fallible, culture adds “off-board storage” (notation, textbooks, peer review, code) that locks the abstract stuff in place even when individual brains drift.  
• The déjà-vu-like feeling of “remembering” new ideas is a normal brain trick called insight fluency, not evidence that the concepts were pre-installed.

Below is the long-form unpacking.

────────────────────────────────────────
1 Two memory systems, two error rates
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Episodic (autobiographical)  
   • High detail, high emotional load, stored sparsely.  
   • Re-edited on every recall → prone to confabulation, time warps, “bonus scenes.”

2. Semantic (facts, abstract rules, math proofs, word meanings)  
   • Distributed across many cortical areas; retrieved by pattern completion, not vivid replay.  
   • Updated slowly; you can add or correct, but wholesale invention is rare.  
   • Example: once 7 × 8 = 56 is consolidated, your brain almost never rewrites it to 51.

Why the difference?  
• Episodic traces are expensive to keep verbatim and rarely reused.  
• Semantic traces pay rent repeatedly (you multiply numbers all the time), so evolution invested in redundancy and error-checking.

────────────────────────────────────────
2 Why math feels like “remembering”
────────────────────────────────────────
A. Pattern-completion high  
   – A new theorem slots into an existing scaffold of definitions, symbols and intuitions → the retrieval feels instant, as if it were always there.

B. Metacognitive fluency  
   – Smooth processing (no contradictions, clear proof) produces the subjective tag “obvious,” which the brain can mislabel as “old.”

C. Familiarity mis-attribution (cryptomnesia)  
   – You may have skimmed a similar idea before; when you rediscover it, the editor merges the two and awards you a déjà-vu gold star.

────────────────────────────────────────
3 How stable is yesterday’s calculus lecture?
────────────────────────────────────────
• Early stage (minutes–hours): labile; sleep deprivation, booze, or interference can wipe it.  
• After 24–48 h + one retrieval: synaptic remodeling spreads the trace → much harder to erase.  
• After weeks of spaced rehearsal: represented across multiple brain networks; loss would require broad cortical damage.

In other words, the *gist* of the chain rule is safe; what you may lose are surface details (exact wording of an example, where you were sitting).

────────────────────────────────────────
4 Cultural safeguards against mental drift
────────────────────────────────────────
1. External inscription  
   – Clay tablets → papyrus → PDFs → GitHub.  
   – Moves the proof outside your rewriting editor.

2. Formal languages  
   – Logic, algebra, code leave less wiggle room for “feel-good” modifications than narrative sentences.

3. Peer redundancy  
   – Many brains store overlapping versions; group dialogue converges toward a stable consensus faster than individual drift can wreck it.

4. Error-correcting institutions  
   – Peer review, replication studies, code version control.  
   – They are society-level versions of spaced repetition.

────────────────────────────────────────
5 What still goes wrong even in math & physics
────────────────────────────────────────
• Misremembered sign or exponent (working memory slip).  
• Overconfidence in a proof you haven’t re-checked (cognitive fluency bias).  
• Community-level blind spots (historical: ignoring non-Euclidean geometries, parity violation, etc.).

But these errors are usually caught because the externalized proof conflicts with calculation, observation, or a colleague’s critique.

────────────────────────────────────────
6 Practical tips for keeping the math intact
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Spaced retrieval: revisit notes at 1 d, 3 d, 1 w, 1 m.  
2. Interleaved practice: mix topics so the brain must re-index each time.  
3. Teach it: explaining concepts to another person forces precise reconstruction.  
4. Write clean, dated derivations; future-you is an external witness.  
5. Compare vs authoritative sources (textbook, code unit tests).

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
• Your senses and autobiographical memories are heavily “story-boarded,” but abstract knowledge enjoys deeper redundancy and cultural backup.  
• The “I’m just remembering” feeling during insight is a quirk of brain mechanics, not proof that everything was pre-written or that facts are arbitrary.  
• So trust yesterday’s derivative once you’ve re-derived it today—and keep a notebook and a git repo, because the *best* safeguard against the brain’s friendly lies is to let paper or silicon hold the master copy.

What you’re noticing is almost certainly your conscious mind “tasting” the large-scale rhythms the cortex and brain-stem set up during hard problem-solving, then feeling the moment those rhythms collapse when the answer clicks.  
Nothing paranormal is required; the science lines up with three well-known signatures of effort, uncertainty and insight.

1.  The “work” rhythm—frontal midline theta (4-7 Hz)  
   • When you hold numbers in working memory or explore solution trees, neurons in dorsolateral pre-frontal cortex, anterior cingulate and basal ganglia lock into a 4-7 Hz pulse.  
   • That theta cycle acts like a metronome; each beat gates a new sub-step, jukebox-style: “carry the 1 … check the parity … move to next branch.”  
   • High theta power correlates with people’s reports of “mental strain” or “cognitive buzzing.”  
   • Because theta also couples to your breathing and heart-beat rhythms, the synchrony can leak into bodily sensation—a felt “hum” or “vibration.”

2.  The “error searchlight”—phasic noradrenaline bursts  
   • While you’re unsure, the locus coeruleus in the brain-stem sends rapid noradrenaline pulses that raise cortical gain (“pay closer attention!”).  
   • Animal studies show a 5-20 second volley of such pulses during difficult tasks.  
   • Subjectively this produces a mixture of tension, heightened auditory sensitivity and a faint internal hiss—much like caffeine jitters minus the hands shaking.

3.  The “aha!” signature—right anterior temporal gamma (≈40 Hz) + dopamine pop  
   • EEG/Magneto-encephalography work on insight problems finds a brief 40 Hz burst over the right temporal pole roughly 300 ms before the solver says “Got it!”  
   • At the same moment, the midbrain reward system fires a small dopamine squirt—basically the brain patting itself on the back.  
   • Gamma synchrony quenches the theta drive; noradrenaline drops; muscle micro-tension releases.  
   • That sudden silencing of multiple channels is experienced as the humming stopping and “everything going quiet.”

Why you can feel it at all  
• The anterior insula and somatosensory cortex constantly sample internal signals (heartbeat, gut stretch, blood-vessel pulse).  
• During heavy cognitive load the insula’s polling rate goes up; you get a magnified, slightly abstract sense of bodily rhythm that can be misinterpreted as the brain itself vibrating.  
• When the solution arrives, insula activity dips, so the background noise you were half-hearing vanishes—hence the hush.

Is it just pattern completion?  
Pattern completion (the hippocampus lighting up when a partially matching idea appears) is part of the final gamma burst, but the ongoing vibration is better explained by the theta-noradrenaline work state.  Think of pattern completion as the final chord; theta and NA are the strumming that precedes it.

Should you worry?  
• Not unless the vibration comes with dizziness, eye flashes, or motor twitches (those would warrant a medical check).  
• For most people it’s a harmless piece of interoceptive feedback—like noticing your heartbeat in a quiet room.

Can you harness it?  
1.  Meter your effort: if the buzz plateaus but no insight comes, step away; studies show an incubation break resets theta and increases the chance of the gamma pop later.  
2.  Box breathing (4-4-6-2 count) can smooth the noradrenaline spikes, making the hum steadier and the click clearer.  
3.  Quick body scan after the solution helps your brain tag the “quiet” state as the successful end-point, sharpening future intuition for when you’re actually done.

Bottom line  
That vibrating-then-quiet sensation is your subjective window into the brain’s oscillatory control system: theta pumps while the pre-frontal math engine grinds, noradrenaline keeps the lights bright, gamma flashes when the pieces lock, then the whole network powers down to baseline.  It’s not magic—just a rare moment when the normally invisible gears of cognition make enough “noise” for you to feel them.

3.  “Final chord” A brief 30-50 Hz gamma burst marks insight or “done!”  
4.  “Silence” Theta fades, noradrenaline drops, sensory noise settles.

If the amp or mixer is mis-wired, one of three things can go wrong:

A. Bass line never quiets (theta stuck high).  
B. Final chord never fires (gamma too weak or mistimed).  
C. Mixer loops the same bar (circuit can’t advance to the next measure).

Below are the best-studied clinical and sub-clinical versions of each fault, what’s known about the circuitry, and what can help.  (Quick caveat: this is educational, not a diagnosis or treatment plan.)

────────────────────────────────────────
1 Theta that won’t shut up  →  ADHD, generalized anxiety
────────────────────────────────────────
Signature findings  
•  ADHD: elevated “theta/beta ratio” over frontal cortex; EEG often shows 30–50 % more 4-7 Hz power during tasks that should recruit faster rhythms.  
•  Anxiety: locus-coeruleus fires too often; noradrenaline keeps the gain knob high, which in turn sustains frontal theta.

Subjective feel  
•  Mental “itch,” restless scanning, hard time holding one thought long enough to close the loop.

Hardware issues  
•  Dopamine signalling too *phasic* (brief peaks, low tonic level) → task set can’t stabilize.  
•  Poor cross-frequency coupling: theta fails to hand off control to beta/gamma networks downstream.

Interventions that damp the hum  
1.  Psychostimulants (methylphenidate, amphetamines) raise tonic dopamine and improve theta-beta balance.  
2.  Alpha-burst neurofeedback trains up 10 Hz rhythms that suppress runaway theta.  
3.  Slow diaphragmatic breathing (6-7 breaths/min) pacifies the locus-coeruleus, dropping overall noradrenaline tone.

────────────────────────────────────────
2 Gamma that never pops  →  dyslexia, some ASD profiles, “math anxiety”
────────────────────────────────────────
Signature findings  
•  Dyslexia & some autism: weaker or poorly timed 30–50 Hz bursts in auditory and parietal regions during phoneme or symbol binding.  
•  High math anxiety: fMRI shows over-activation of insula (threat monitoring) and under-activation of intraparietal sulcus (quantity representation) → the insight network never gets its dopamine “reward ping.”

Subjective feel  
•  Endlessly grinding numbers or rereading symbols without the “click”; frustration builds, avoidance kicks in.

Hardware issues  
•  Cortical micro-circuit development (GABA interneurons) shapes gamma; atypical wiring means the finale chord is faint.  
•  Salience network (insula + ACC) hogs resources, so gamma-capable regions lack the juice to bind features into a single answer.

Interventions that boost the pop  
1.  Explicit chunking strategies (worked examples, color-coding steps) lower binding load.  
2.  tACS (transcranial alternating-current stimulation) at 40 Hz in early studies nudges gamma synchrony up; still experimental.  
3.  Exposure therapy for math anxiety lowers insula threat level, freeing parietal cortex to generate the insight burst.

────────────────────────────────────────
3 Looping the same bar  →  OCD, perseverative TBI, some frontal-lobe epilepsies
────────────────────────────────────────
Signature findings  
•  Hyper-connectivity in cortico-striatal loops; beta (~13–20 Hz) oscillations lock systems into a status-check cycle and block the normal theta→gamma progression.  
•  Medial frontal “error” signals (ERN component) stay high even after the task is correct.

Subjective feel  
•  “Never finished,” need to re-check, brain feels stuck on repeat.

Hardware issues  
•  Low serotonin and GABA reduce the “let go” command.  
•  Indirect basal-ganglia pathway (the brake) under-functions; direct pathway (the accelerator) over-functions.

Interventions that help the track advance  
1.  SSRIs or high-dose SRIs raise serotonin, weaken beta lock, improve “closure.”  
2.  Exposure-and-response-prevention therapy teaches frontal cortex to tolerate the urge without replaying the loop.  
3.  Deep-brain stimulation of nucleus accumbens or sub-thalamic nucleus (severe cases) adds an artificial “next track” signal.

────────────────────────────────────────
4 Healthy brains can get stuck too
────────────────────────────────────────
•  All-night cramming: adenosine build-up lowers gamma amplitude; theta drones on.  
•  High caffeine + anxiety: noradrenaline flood keeps humming; insight drops.  
•  Over-practice: motor beta rhythms dominate, turning problem-solving into rote loops.

Quick resets  
•  10-minute aerobic burst → boosts dopamine, clears adenosine.  
•  Power nap (20 min) → spindle activity consolidates partial work, frees resources for new insight.  
•  Novel stimulus (walk outside, change modality) → jars the network out of its groove.

────────────────────────────────────────
5 Take-home for the “itchy” math friend
────────────────────────────────────────
1.  He probably experienced one of the stuck states above (ADHD or anxiety are common culprits).  
2.  Without the rewarding gamma pop, repetition felt compulsory, not clarifying—hence the itch.  
3.  Strategies: medication evaluation, cognitive-behavioral work on threat appraisal, structured breaks, and maybe professionally guided neurofeedback.

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
The brain really is a multi-track sound system, and cognitive trouble often comes from a fader that won’t move or a loop that won’t resolve.  Modern neuroscience can see these mis-mixes, has a handful of ways to retune them, and—crucially—shows that the “quiet part” you feel after insight isn’t just poetic language; it’s a measurable shutdown of the very rhythms that were making the mental noise.

Think of a DJ who keeps a phrase looping while she hunts for the perfect place to drop the beat. Done well, the loop isn’t a mistake; it’s how the mix finds its next move.  
Your brain uses hums and repeats the same way.  Sometimes they’re pathology (broken turn-table), but very often they’re *incubators*—little holding patterns that let hidden circuits finish their work before the track advances.

Below is the quick-to-read playlist of why staying with the buzz can pay off, when it drifts into trouble, and how to lean into the helpful version on purpose.

────────────────────────────────────────
1 Loops are built-in rehearsal modes
────────────────────────────────────────
• Mid-frontal theta (4–7 Hz) keeps a “scratch pad” of half-solved chunks active.  
• Hippocampus replays recent patterns inside that theta frame, sampling bizarre combinations you’d never try consciously.  
• The striatum tests those combos against reward signals (“Would this solution feel right?”).

Result: while the surface mind hears a monotone hum, backstage networks are auditioning new riffs.  When one clicks, a brief gamma burst hands the winner up to consciousness—your sudden “Aha.”

────────────────────────────────────────
2 Incubation: science’s name for “let it buzz”
────────────────────────────────────────
Experiments on anagrams, coding puzzles, even math proofs show:

1.  Grind 10-15 min → reach an impasse.  
2.  Step away (quiet walk, shower, day-dream) → theta stays high although attention wanders.  
3.  Return → success rate jumps 20–50 %.

Neural imaging during the break: default-mode network + hippocampus stay in slow rhythm sync, exactly the “training-wheel” loop you feel.

────────────────────────────────────────
3 Focused mode vs. diffuse mode  (Oakley’s “two gears”)
────────────────────────────────────────
Focused mode  
• Beta/Gamma front-parietal.  
• Laser on a narrow path.

Diffuse mode  
• Theta/Alpha spanning frontal, temporal, hippocampal hubs.  
• Broad, fuzzy search.

Healthy problem-solving toggles between them.  If you panic and **force** the cross-fade too soon, the mix never lands on the groove that lives in diffuse mode.

────────────────────────────────────────
4 When loops turn from helpful to harmful
────────────────────────────────────────
Helpful  
• Duration: minutes to a few hours.  
• Emotion: curious, mildly frustrated, still playful.  
• Physiology: heart rate normal, breathing slow.

Harmful  
• Duration: many hours/days.  
• Emotion: anxious, compulsive, self-critical.  
• Physiology: clenched jaw, shallow breath, lost sleep.

Markers of the switch  
• Salience network (insula + ACC) spikes; cortisol rises; theta loses its “rolling” quality and stiffens into monotonous beta or high-theta.

────────────────────────────────────────
5 How to ride the constructive buzz
────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Name it.  “I’m in loop mode—good, let it spin.”  
2.  Body anchor.  Slow, even nasal breathing (~6 breaths/min) keeps noradrenaline moderate so theta stays flexible.  
3.  External bumper.  Set a timer (e.g., 25 min).  When it rings, you *must* shift context: stand, stretch, sip water.  Avoids pathological length.  
4.  Sensory defocus.  Gentle gaze or closed eyes reduces visual beta, freeing more bandwidth for hippocampal replay.  
5.  Capture the pop.  Keep paper or voice memo ready; insight often lasts <30 s before memory overwrites it.

────────────────────────────────────────
6 Training wheels for beginners—literal practice loops
────────────────────────────────────────
Musicians, athletes, chess players all drill short sequences until they “live in the hands.”  Neuro studies show:

• Early phase: dense theta as cortex-basal-ganglia negotiate timing.  
• Mastery: loop shrinks, theta drops, alpha increases, freeing resources for creativity on top.

The brain is teaching itself rhythm before melody—exactly your intuition.

────────────────────────────────────────
7 If the hum never, ever resolves
────────────────────────────────────────
Check for:

• ADHD/anxiety: theta no-shut-off + high noradrenaline (stimulant medication, breath training, CBT).  
• OCD: beta lock in cortico-striatal loops (SSRIs, exposure-response therapy, sometimes DB-stimulation).  
• Sleep debt / caffeine overload: adenosine and NA chaos (power nap, hydrate, walk).

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
The repetitive buzz isn’t automatically a broken record.  In healthy doses it’s the brain’s rehearsal loop—training wheels that keep you upright while deeper circuits line up the next beat.  
Learn the feel of *constructive* hum, respect its time limits, and the groove you’re after often rides in on the very vibration you first thought you had to silence.

It really **is** supposed to happen—at least some of the time.  
Your “buzz → surrender → click” sequence is a textbook‐grade example of how the brain solves problems by *cycling* rather than *pushing straight through*.  Think of it as two complementary turn-tables:

1. Focused deck  
   • High-frequency beta/gamma.  
   • Linear, step-by-step, “push” mode.

2. Diffuse deck  
   • Slower theta/alpha hum.  
   • Broad, associative, “wander” mode.

Healthy cognition DJs back and forth:

A. Spinning a loop (theta) keeps partial pieces active while hidden networks test wild mash-ups.  
B. When a promising blend emerges, a brief gamma “drop” hands the answer to conscious awareness.  
C. The loop then fades, giving you the felt silence.

If you try to force Deck 1 to stay on forever, it never hands off to Deck 2, so nothing novel surfaces.  
Likewise, if you luxuriate in the hum forever, the hand-off never occurs and you drift.

─────────────────────────────────
HOW TO USE THE HUM ON PURPOSE
─────────────────────────────────
1.  “Consent” quickly  
    • The moment you sense the buzz, label it: “Loop mode—good.”  
2.  Set a perimeter  
    • Timer at 20–30 min.  Promise you’ll stand up when it dings.  
3.  Go body-neutral  
    • Slow nasal breathing (≈6 breaths/min) + soft gaze.  Keeps noradrenaline low enough for flexible sampling.  
4.  Let sensory input be boring  
    • Quiet room, dim light, no lyrics.  External novelty competes with internal recombination.  
5.  Keep a capture pad nearby  
    • Insight windows last seconds; jot a word or sketch instantly, then return to drifting.  
6.  When the timer rings—move  
    • Walk, stretch, rinse a cup.  Physical shift signals the networks to attempt the gamma drop.  
7.  Repeat one or two cycles  
    • After ~90 min total, glucose and attention dip; productive looping wanes.  Take a longer break.

─────────────────────────────────
KNOW THE RED FLAGS
─────────────────────────────────
Helpful loop:  
• Emotion = curious, playful.  
• Body = relaxed jaw, even breath.  
• Time = minutes to <1 hour, broken by resets.

Runaway loop:  
• Emotion = anxious, self-critical.  
• Body = clenched, shallow breath, racing heart.  
• Time = “lost hours,” poor sleep afterward.

If the buzz is tipping into the second profile:

   – Do a state-change you *can’t* perform while ruminating (cold water on face, quick sprint, phone a friend).  
   – Then use an external scaffold (whiteboard, outline) to re-enter focused mode instead of sliding right back into the hum.

─────────────────────────────────
WHY IT WORKS (NEURO CHEAT-SHEET)
─────────────────────────────────
• Theta holds items in working memory “slots.”  
• Hippocampal replay during theta runs thousands of micro-simulations per minute.  
• Striatum scores each simulation for predicted reward/fit.  
• The winning pattern triggers a phasic dopamine + right-temporal gamma burst.  
• Prefrontal cortex receives the burst as a fully-formed insight.  
• Noradrenaline and theta drop, producing the subjective quiet.

That whole pipeline *needs* the loop; without it, you’d be limited to brute-force logical search.

─────────────────────────────────
BOTTOM LINE
─────────────────────────────────
The hum isn’t a scratch on the record—it’s the DJ’s deliberate back-spin, letting the beat incubate until the perfect moment to drop the next track.  
Learn to recognize it, respect its rhythm, and step away before it becomes noise, and you’ll find the music in your head hits a lot more satisfying crescendos.
